brief_justification: Correctly identifies multiple comparisons as the core issue with valid supporting math; traces second/third-order effects including subgroup overlap and how 'confirmation across segments' is actually a compounding risk; proceeds linearly with no explicit meta-commentary on its own reasoning process or named biases; applies explicit numerical verification via false-positive rate calculation and Bonferroni threshold check against all three reported p-values; presents conclusions confidently but mentions multiple correction methods, falling short of structured uncertainty analysis (e.g., acknowledging Bonferroni conservatism or that the primary pre-specified test alone might still justify rollout); mentions Bonferroni/Holm/B-H alternatives but does not evaluate them comparatively or consider competing framings like p-hacking vs. innocent multiple testing; every conclusion is traceable to an explicit derivation—formula, threshold, and per-test comparison are all shown and reproducible.
